trigger userKey

trigger userKey

Trigger for responding to user-defined key sequences as defined in the Keyboard Translation Table (KTT) or defined in the usys.ini section named as userkeys.

Declaration:

trigger userKey

Applies to: Application Shell, Form
Activation: Activated in response to the ^USER_KEY request. Uniface first tries to activate the component-level trigger. If it is empty, or returns a value >=0 (but not 99), the application-level trigger is activated.

The userKey trigger is not activated if the structure editor is not available, for example if it is waiting for a response to an askmess, if there is a menu active, if a panel is showing, and so on.
Default behavior: None
Behavior upon completion: If $status after the component-level trigger is:
  • >=0, but not 99—the application-level userKey trigger is activated (without a beep).
  • <0 —the application-level userKey trigger is not activated (with a beep).
  • 99—the application-level userKey trigger is not activated (without a beep).

None

Description

The userKey trigger is used to define the processing associated with the user-defined key sequences. These are defined in the keyboard translation table using the ^USER_KEY definition or in the usys.ini file section named as userkeys.

When userkeys are defined in the keyboard translation table using the ^USER_KEY definition, the identification character for this user key sequence becomes available in $char. All characters between the identification character and the terminator code defined for the user key are stored in $result, provided that no more than 61 characters are entered. Characters after the 61st character are ignored until the defined terminator character sequence is encountered.

Code example

Example 1: Using the Keyboard translation table

The definition in the keyboard translation table can be the following three lines for CTRL+K, CTRL+L, CTRL+P respectively:

^130^107 ^USER_KEY^150

^130^108 ^USER_KEY^156

^130^112 ^USER_KEY^162

In the userkey trigger, we can now define the following: 

trigger userkey
message/info "User key %%$char pressed!"

if ($char=150)
  do some work here
endif

Example 2: Using the usys.ini section named userkeys

When the userkey is defined in the usys.ini file in the chapter named [userkeys] while there is no menu in the current component with the same accelerator in a menu, the userkey trigger gets fired with in $result the name of the userkey.

In the following example it is fileopen when the user presses the control key plus ‘o’:

For userkeys definition:
[userkeys] 
fileopen=CTRL+O
For trigger userkey definition:
trigger userkey 
if ($result=”fileopen”) 
  activate “mycomponent”.lets-open-a-file() 
endif
Note:
  • Userkeys are ignored when the current component has no trigger userkey and application level trigger userkey defined.
  • The userkeys are only passed through to the 4GL when the trigger is available in the current context.
  • The default keys for labels with the same key combination as a userkey will keep working and also fire the userkey trigger.
  • For example, an associated label with Hel%lo will set the focus to the associated field when pressing the Alt+l.
  • If this key combination is also in the userkeys section or defined in the KTT, then both the focus shifts to the associated field and the trigger is fired if the trigger is available.
  • The userkey section can contain the same keys as explained in the [accelerators] topic. For more details, see [accelerators].

Precedence rules

  • Menu accelerators, when a menu is available in the current component
  • userkeys defined in the usys.ini when the trigger is available
  • userkey definitions in the keyboard translation table
Related Links
  • User Key Definitions

    User Key Definitions

  • $char

    In a Unifield, return the Uniface character code for the key that activated a trigger.

  • $result

    Return the result of certain ProcScript commands and tree widget events.

  • Reserved Keys on Microsoft Windows

    It is possible to redefine the behavior of keys by editing the MSWINX keyboard translation table or using your own customized version. Such changes will affect the behavior of Unifields, but may be ignored by widgets.
